title: Understanding cancer at a genetic level with AI
summary: Google DeepMind researchers and Dr. Daudi Jjingo's team at Makerere University use AlphaFold, AlphaGenome, and Antigravity to identify genetic targets for breast cancer vaccine development in Uganda. The team analyzed protein binding sites to narrow 15,000 candidates to 15 viable targets for validation.
writeup: A Ugandan researcher uses AlphaFold to identify protein targets for a breast cancer vaccine, working on a disease that strikes 1 in 12 Ugandan women and is caught later and survived less often than in high-income settings. AlphaFold narrows 15,000 candidate binding sites to 15 viable targets, collapsing work that previously required abroad-based infrastructure to a laptop and server connection. The through-line is concrete: democratized compute tools are enabling in-country translational research that could produce a locally relevant cancer vaccine.
key_points: Breast cancer in Uganda presents at earlier ages and with lower survival rates than global averages, largely because screening happens only after symptoms appear rather than at a genetic or protein level.
AlphaFold, combined with an additional bioinformatics tool, enabled the team to reduce 15,000 candidate vaccine target sites to just 15 high-priority proteins expressed in breast cancer patients.

The capital cost reduction from AlphaFold means researchers no longer need to send this work abroad: a laptop and server connection now provide sufficient computational power for structural proteomics.

If the 15 identified protein targets are validated in the lab, they become candidates for vaccine development, representing a direct pipeline from computational biology to public health intervention in Uganda.

Google DeepMind's AlphaFold is described as democratizing the kind of science possible in lower-resource settings, shifting the bottleneck from hardware and institutional access to the research question itself.
